text.Rotherham: Then came round ten young men who bare Joab’s armour,—and smote Absolom, and slew him.

text.NRSV: Then Joab sounded the trumpet, and the troops came back from pursuing Israel, for Joab restrained the troops.
text.Rotherham: Then Joab blew with a horn, and the people returned from pursuing Israel,—for Joab had restrained the people.

text.NRSV: They took Absalom, and threw him into a great pit in the forest, and raised over him a very great heap of stones. Meanwhile all the Israelites fled to their homes.
text.Rotherham: And they took Absolom, and cast him, in the forest, into a large pit, and raised up over him a very great heap of stones,—and, all Israel, fled, every man to his home.

text.NRSV: Now Absalom in his lifetime had taken and erected for himself a pillar in the King’s Valley, for he said, “I have no son to keep my name in remembrance”; he called the pillar by his own name. It is called Absalom’s Monument to this day.
text.Rotherham: But, Absolom, had taken, and raised up for himself, in his lifetime, the pillar that is in the king’s vale, for he said, I have no son, to keep in remembrance my name, so he called the pillar after his own name, and it is called Absolom’s monument unto this day.

text.NRSV: Then Ahimaaz son of Zadok said, “Let me run, and carry tidings to the king that the Lord has delivered him from the power of his enemies.”
text.Rotherham: Then, Ahimaaz, son of Zadok, said, Let me run, I pray thee, and carry tidings unto the king,—how that Yahweh hath vindicated him, at the hand of his enemies.

text.NRSV: Joab said to him, “You are not to carry tidings today; you may carry tidings another day, but today you shall not do so, because the king’s son is dead.”
text.Rotherham: And Joab said to him—Not a man to bear tidings, art thou this day, but thou shalt bear tidings another day,—but, this day, shalt thou not bear tidings, for this cause, that, the king’s son, is dead.

text.NRSV: Then Joab said to a Cushite, “Go, tell the king what you have seen.” The Cushite bowed before Joab, and ran.
text.Rotherham: Then said Joab to a Cushite, Go tell the king, what thou hast seen. And the Cushite bowed himself down to Joab, and ran.

text.NRSV: Then Ahimaaz son of Zadok said again to Joab, “Come what may, let me also run after the Cushite.” And Joab said, “Why will you run, my son, seeing that you will have no reward for the tidings?”
text.Rotherham: Then, yet again, said Ahimaaz son of Zadok unto Joab. But, be what may, do, I pray thee, let, me also, run, after the Cushite. And Joab said—Wherefore is it that, thou, wouldst run, my son, when, thou, hast no tidings of, any profit?

text.NRSV: “Come what may,” he said, “I will run.” So he said to him, “Run.” Then Ahimaaz ran by the way of the Plain, and outran the Cushite.
text.Rotherham: But, be what may, I will run. So he said to him—Run. Then ran Ahimaaz by the way of the plain, and got beyond the Cushite.

text.NRSV: Now David was sitting between the two gates. The sentinel went up to the roof of the gate by the wall, and when he looked up, he saw a man running alone.
text.Rotherham: Now, David, was sitting between the two gates,—and the watchman went on to the top of the gate-house, upon the wall, and lifted up his eyes, and looked, and lo! a man, running alone.

text.NRSV: The sentinel shouted and told the king. The king said, “If he is alone, there are tidings in his mouth.” He kept coming, and drew near.
text.Rotherham: So the watchman called out, and told the king. And the king said, If he is alone, there are tidings in his mouth. And he came on nearer and nearer.

text.NRSV: Then the sentinel saw another man running; and the sentinel called to the gatekeeper and said, “See, another man running alone!” The king said, “He also is bringing tidings.”
text.Rotherham: Then saw the watchman another man, running, so he called out unto the porter, Lo! a man, running alone. And the king said. This one also, beareth tidings.

text.NRSV: The sentinel said, “I think the running of the first one is like the running of Ahimaaz son of Zadok.” The king said, “He is a good man, and comes with good tidings.”
text.Rotherham: Then said the watchman, It seemeth, to me, that, the running of the foremost, is like the running of Ahimaaz son of Zadok. And the king said, A good man, is he, and, with good tidings, he cometh.

text.NRSV: Then Ahimaaz cried out to the king, “All is well!” He prostrated himself before the king with his face to the ground, and said, “Blessed be the Lord your God, who has delivered up the men who raised their hand against my lord the king.”
text.Rotherham: Then called out Ahimaaz, and said unto the king, Peace! And he bowed himself down to the king, with his face to the earth,—and said—Blessed, be Yahweh thy God, who hath surrendered the men who were lifting up their hand, against my lord the king.

text.NRSV: The king said, “Is it well with the young man Absalom?” Ahimaaz answered, “When Joab sent your servant, I saw a great tumult, but I do not know what it was.”
text.Rotherham: And the king said, Is it, well, with the young man—Absolom? Then said Ahimaaz—I saw a great crowd, when Joab sent the king’s servant and [me] thy servant, but I knew not what [it meant] .

text.NRSV: The king said, “Turn aside, and stand here.” So he turned aside, and stood still.
text.Rotherham: And the king said, Aside! stand, here. So he turned aside, and stood.

text.NRSV: Then the Cushite came; and the Cushite said, “Good tidings for my lord the king! For the Lord has vindicated you today, delivering you from the power of all who rose up against you.”
text.Rotherham: Then lo! the Cushite, coming in,—and the Cushite said—Tidings, getteth my lord the king, how that Yahweh hath vindicated thee to-day, at the hand of all them who had risen up against thee.

text.NRSV: The king said to the Cushite, “Is it well with the young man Absalom?” The Cushite answered, “May the enemies of my lord the king, and all who rise up to do you harm, be like that young man.”
text.Rotherham: And the king said unto the Cushite—Is it, well, with the young man—Absolom? Then said the Cushite—Be, like the young man, the enemies of my lord the king, and all who have risen up against thee, for harm.

text.NRSV: The king was deeply moved, and went up to the chamber over the gate, and wept; and as he went, he said, “O my son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom! Would I had died instead of you, O Absalom, my son, my son!”
text.Rotherham: Then was the king deeply moved, and went up to the chamber over the gate, and wept,—and, thus, he said as he went—O my son Absolom, my son—my son—Absolom! could, I, but have died in thy stead, O Absolom, my son—my son!

text.NRSV: It was told Joab, “The king is weeping and mourning for Absalom.”
text.Rotherham: And it was told Joab,—Lo! the king, is weeping and mourning over Absolom.

text.NRSV: So the victory that day was turned into mourning for all the troops; for the troops heard that day, “The king is grieving for his son.”
text.Rotherham: So the victory, on that day, was turned into mourning, with all the people,—for the people heard, on that day, saying, The king is distressed for his son.

text.NRSV: The troops stole into the city that day as soldiers steal in who are ashamed when they flee in battle.
text.Rotherham: And the people stole away, on that day, to go into the city,—as people steal away who are put to shame, when they flee in battle.

text.NRSV: The king covered his face, and the king cried with a loud voice, “O my son Absalom, O Absalom, my son, my son!”
text.Rotherham: But, the king, muffled his face, and the king made outcry, with a loud voice,—O my son Absolom, O Absolom, my son, my son!

text.NRSV: Then Joab came into the house to the king, and said, “Today you have covered with shame the faces of all your officers who have saved your life today, and the lives of your sons and your daughters, and the lives of your wives and your concubines,
text.Rotherham: Then came Joab unto the king, in the house, —and said—Thou hast, to-day, covered with shame the faces of all thy servants, who have rescued thy life to-day, and the lives of thy sons and thy daughters, and the lives of thy wives, and the lives of thy concubines;

text.NRSV: for love of those who hate you and for hatred of those who love you. You have made it clear today that commanders and officers are nothing to you; for I perceive that if Absalom were alive and all of us were dead today, then you would be pleased.
text.Rotherham: by loving them who hated thee, and hating them who loved thee,—for thou hast declared, to-day, that, nothing to thee, are princes or servants, for I perceive, to-day, that, if, Absolom, had lived, and, all we, to-day had died, that, then, it had been right in thine eyes.

text.NRSV: Now then, go out and speak kindly to your servants; for I swear by the Lord, if you do not go, not a man will stay with you this night; and this will be worse for you than any disaster that has come upon you from your youth until now.”
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, rise—go forth, and speak unto the heart of thy servants,—for, by Yahweh, have I sworn, that, if thou do not go forth, not a man shall tarry with thee to-night, and this will be to thee, a greater misfortune, than all the misfortune that hath come upon thee from thy youth until now.

text.NRSV: Then the king got up and took his seat in the gate. The troops were all told, “See, the king is sitting in the gate”; and all the troops came before the king. Meanwhile, Israel had fled every man to his home.
text.Rotherham: So the king arose, and took his seat in the gate,—and, to all the people, was it told, saying—Lo! the king, is sitting in the gate. Then came all the people before the king, but, Israel, had fled every man to his home.

text.NRSV: All the people were disputing throughout all the tribes of Israel, saying, “The king delivered us from the hand of our enemies, and saved us from the hand of the Philistines; and now he has fled out of the land because of Absalom.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass that all the people were reproaching one another, throughout all the tribes of Israel, saying,—the king, delivered us out of the hand of our enemies, and, he, rescued us out of the hand of the Philistines, but, now, he hath fled out of the land, away from Absolom;

text.NRSV: But Absalom, whom we anointed over us, is dead in battle. Now therefore why do you say nothing about bringing the king back?”
text.Rotherham: and, Absolom, whom we anointed over us, hath died in the battle. Now, therefore, why are, ye, silent as to bringing back the king?

text.NRSV: King David sent this message to the priests Zadok and Abiathar: “Say to the elders of Judah, ‘Why should you be the last to bring the king back to his house? The talk of all Israel has come to the king.
text.Rotherham: And, King David, sent unto Zadok and unto Abiathar the priests, saying, Speak ye unto the elders of Judah, saying, Wherefore should ye be behindhand, in bringing back the king unto his home,—seeing that, the speech of all Israel, hath come unto the king, regarding his home?

text.NRSV: You are my kin, you are my bone and my flesh; why then should you be the last to bring back the king?’
text.Rotherham: Mine own brethren, are ye, my bone and my flesh, are ye,—wherefore then should ye be behindhand in bringing back the king?

text.NRSV: And say to Amasa, ‘Are you not my bone and my flesh? So may God do to me, and more, if you are not the commander of my army from now on, in place of Joab.’”
text.Rotherham: And, unto Amasa, shall ye say, Art not, thou, my bone and my flesh? So, let God do to me, and, so, let him add, if thou become not, prince of the army, before me continually, instead of Joab.

text.NRSV: Amasa swayed the hearts of all the people of Judah as one, and they sent word to the king, “Return, both you and all your servants.”
text.Rotherham: Thus bowed he the heart of all the men of Judah, as one man,—and they sent unto the king, Return, thou, and all thy servants.

text.NRSV: So the king came back to the Jordan; and Judah came to Gilgal to meet the king and to bring him over the Jordan.
text.Rotherham: Then the king returned, and came as far as the Jordan,—and, Judah, came to Gilgal, to go and meet the king, to escort the king over the Jordan.

text.NRSV: Shimei son of Gera, the Benjaminite, from Bahurim, hurried to come down with the people of Judah to meet King David;
text.Rotherham: Then hastened Shimei, son of Gera, the Benjamite, who was of Bahurim,—and came down, with the men of Judah, to meet King David.

text.NRSV: with him were a thousand people from Benjamin. And Ziba, the servant of the house of Saul, with his fifteen sons and his twenty servants, rushed down to the Jordan ahead of the king,
text.Rotherham: And, a thousand men, were with him, out of Benjamin, Ziba also, servant of the house of Saul, and his fifteen sons and twenty servants, with him,—and they went through the Jordan, before the king.

text.NRSV: and they crossed the ford to bring over the king’s household, and to do his pleasure. Shimei son of Gera fell down before the king, as he was about to cross the Jordan,
text.Rotherham: But the ferry-boat kept crossing, to bring over the household of the king, and to do what was good in his eyes. And, Shimei, son of Gera, fell down before the king, when he had passed over the Jordan;

text.NRSV: and said to the king, “May my lord not hold me guilty or remember how your servant did wrong on the day my lord the king left Jerusalem; may the king not bear it in mind.
text.Rotherham: and he said unto the king—Let not my lord impute to me iniquity, neither do thou remember the perverseness of thy servant, on the day that thou wentest out, my lord O king, from Jerusalem, that the king should lay it upon his heart.

text.NRSV: For your servant knows that I have sinned; therefore, see, I have come this day, the first of all the house of Joseph to come down to meet my lord the king.”
text.Rotherham: For thy servant doth know, that, I, sinned, —lo! therefore, I have arrived to-day, as the first of all the house of Joseph, to come down to meet my lord the king.

text.NRSV: Abishai son of Zeruiah answered, “Shall not Shimei be put to death for this, because he cursed the Lord’s anointed?”
text.Rotherham: Then responded Abishai, son of Zeruiah, and said, For this, shall not Shimei be put to death, for that he cursed the Anointed of Yahweh?

text.NRSV: But David said, “What have I to do with you, you sons of Zeruiah, that you should today become an adversary to me? Shall anyone be put to death in Israel this day? For do I not know that I am this day king over Israel?”
text.Rotherham: But David said, What have I in common with you, ye sons of Zeruiah, for ye would become to me, to-day, a very traitor! Shall there, to-day, be put to death a man in Israel? for do I not know, that, to-day, I, am king over Israel?

text.NRSV: The king said to Shimei, “You shall not die.” And the king gave him his oath.
text.Rotherham: Then said the king unto Shimei—Thou shalt not die. And the king sware to him.

text.NRSV: Mephibosheth grandson of Saul came down to meet the king; he had not taken care of his feet, or trimmed his beard, or washed his clothes, from the day the king left until the day he came back in safety.
text.Rotherham: And, Mephibosheth, son of Saul, came down to meet the king,—he had neither dressed his feet, nor trimmed his beard, nor, his clothes, had he washed, from the day the king departed, until the day that he entered in peace.

text.NRSV: When he came from Jerusalem to meet the king, the king said to him, “Why did you not go with me, Mephibosheth?”
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when he entered Jerusalem to meet the king, that the king said unto him, Wherefore wentest thou not with me, Mephibosheth.

text.NRSV: He answered, “My lord, O king, my servant deceived me; for your servant said to him, ‘Saddle a donkey for me, so that I may ride on it and go with the king.’ For your servant is lame.
text.Rotherham: And he said, My lord, O king, my servant, betrayed me, —for thy servant said—I will even saddle me mine ass, that I may ride thereon, and go with the king, for, lame, is thy servant.

text.NRSV: He has slandered your servant to my lord the king. But my lord the king is like the angel of God; do therefore what seems good to you.
text.Rotherham: And he hath slandered thy servant, unto my lord the king,—but, my lord the king, is as a messenger of God, do therefore what is good in thine own eyes.

text.NRSV: For all my father’s house were worthy of death before my lord the king; but you set your servant among those who eat at your table. What further right have I, then, to appeal to the king?”
text.Rotherham: For, when all the house of my father were nothing better than dead men, unto my lord the king, then didst thou set thy servant among them that used to eat at thy table,—what then have I further, by way of right, or to cry out any further unto the king?

text.NRSV: The king said to him, “Why speak any more of your affairs? I have decided: you and Ziba shall divide the land.”
text.Rotherham: Then the king said unto him, Wherefore shouldst thou speak any further of thine affairs? I have said—Thou and Ziba, shall share the land.

text.NRSV: Mephibosheth said to the king, “Let him take it all, since my lord the king has arrived home safely.”
text.Rotherham: And Mephibosheth said unto the king, Even the whole, let him take,—now that my lord the king hath entered, in peace, into his own house.

text.NRSV: Now Barzillai the Gileadite had come down from Rogelim; he went on with the king to the Jordan, to escort him over the Jordan.
text.Rotherham: And, Barzillai the Gileadite, came down from Rogelim,—and passed, with the king, over the Jordan, to escort him over the Jordan.

text.NRSV: Barzillai was a very aged man, eighty years old. He had provided the king with food while he stayed at Mahanaim, for he was a very wealthy man.
text.Rotherham: Now, Barzillai, was very aged, eighty years old,—and, he himself, had sustained the king, throughout his sojourn in Mahanaim, for he was, an exceeding great man.

text.NRSV: The king said to Barzillai, “Come over with me, and I will provide for you in Jerusalem at my side.”
text.Rotherham: So then the king said unto Barzillai,—Thou, come over with me, and I will sustain thee with me, in Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: But Barzillai said to the king, “How many years have I still to live, that I should go up with the king to Jerusalem?
text.Rotherham: But Barzillai said unto the king,—Like unto what, are the days of the years of my life, that I should come up with the king, to Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Today I am eighty years old; can I discern what is pleasant and what is not? Can your servant taste what he eats or what he drinks? Can I still listen to the voice of singing men and singing women? Why then should your servant be an added burden to my lord the king?
text.Rotherham: Eighty years old, am I to-day—could I discern between good and bad? or could thy servant taste what I might eat, and what I might drink? or could I hearken any more to the voice of singing men and singing women? Wherefore, then, should thy servant yet be a burden unto my lord the king?

text.NRSV: Your servant will go a little way over the Jordan with the king. Why should the king recompense me with such a reward?
text.Rotherham: Just a little way, will thy servant pass over the Jordan with the king,—but wherefore should the king recompense me with this reward?

text.NRSV: Please let your servant return, so that I may die in my own town, near the graves of my father and my mother. But here is your servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king; and do for him whatever seems good to you.”
text.Rotherham: Let thy servant, I pray thee, turn back again, that I may die in mine own city, by the grave of my father, and my mother. But here is thy servant Chimham—let him pass over with my lord the king, and do unto him that which may be good in thine eyes.

text.NRSV: The king answered, “Chimham shall go over with me, and I will do for him whatever seems good to you; and all that you desire of me I will do for you.”
text.Rotherham: Then said the king, With me, shall Chimham pass over, and, I, will do unto him that which shall be good in thine eyes,—and, whatsoever thou shalt choose to lay upon me, I will do for thee.

text.NRSV: Then all the people crossed over the Jordan, and the king crossed over; the king kissed Barzillai and blessed him, and he returned to his own home.
text.Rotherham: And, when all the people had passed over the Jordan, then, the king, passed over, —and the king kissed Barzillai, and blessed him, and he returned unto his own place.

text.NRSV: The king went on to Gilgal, and Chimham went on with him; all the people of Judah, and also half the people of Israel, brought the king on his way.
text.Rotherham: And the king passed over to Gilgal, and, Chimham, passed over with him,—and, all the people of Judah, escorted the king, yea moreover, half the people of Israel.

text.NRSV: Then all the people of Israel came to the king, and said to him, “Why have our kindred the people of Judah stolen you away, and brought the king and his household over the Jordan, and all David’s men with him?”
text.Rotherham: Then lo! all the men of Israel, were coming unto the king,—and they said unto the king—Why did our brethren the men of Judah steal thee away, and escort the king and his household over the Jordan, and all the men of David with him?

text.NRSV: All the people of Judah answered the people of Israel, “Because the king is near of kin to us. Why then are you angry over this matter? Have we eaten at all at the king’s expense? Or has he given us any gift?”
text.Rotherham: And all the men of Judah made answer unto the men of Israel—Because the king is, near of kin, unto us, wherefore, then, is it, that ye are angry over this matter? Have we, eaten, at the king’s cost? or hath he, bestowed any gifts, on us?

text.NRSV: But the people of Israel answered the people of Judah, “We have ten shares in the king, and in David also we have more than you. Why then did you despise us? Were we not the first to speak of bringing back our king?” But the words of the people of Judah were fiercer than the words of the people of Israel.
text.Rotherham: And the men of Israel answered the men of Judah, and said—Ten parts, have we in the king, therefore, even in David, have we more right than ye. Why, then, made ye light of us, so that our word was not heard first as to bringing back our king? And, the words of the men of Judah, were fiercer than, the words of the men of Israel.

text.NRSV: Now a scoundrel named Sheba son of Bichri, a Benjaminite, happened to be there. He sounded the trumpet and cried out, “We have no portion in David, no share in the son of Jesse! Everyone to your tents, O Israel!”
text.Rotherham: Now, in that place, there happened to be an abandoned man, whose name, was Sheba son of Bichri, a man of Benjamin,—so he blew a horn, and said—We have no share in David, Nor inheritance have we in the son of Jesse, Every man to his home, O Israel!

text.NRSV: So all the people of Israel withdrew from David and followed Sheba son of Bichri; but the people of Judah followed their king steadfastly from the Jordan to Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: Then went up all the men of Israel from following David, to follow Sheba son of Bichri,—but, the men of Judah, clave unto their king, from the Jordan, even as far as Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: David came to his house at Jerusalem; and the king took the ten concubines whom he had left to look after the house, and put them in a house under guard, and provided for them, but did not go in to them. So they were shut up until the day of their death, living as if in widowhood.
text.Rotherham: And David entered into his own house, in Jerusalem, and the king took the ten women, the concubines whom he had left to keep the house, and put them in ward, and sustained them, but, unto them, went he not in,—so they were shut up until the day of their death, in lifelong widowhood.

text.NRSV: Then the king said to Amasa, “Call the men of Judah together to me within three days, and be here yourself.”
text.Rotherham: Then said the king unto Amasa, Assemble me the men of Judah, within three days,—and, thou, here, take thy stand!

text.NRSV: So Amasa went to summon Judah; but he delayed beyond the set time that had been appointed him.
text.Rotherham: So Amasa went, to assemble Judah,—but he tarried beyond the fixed time, which he had appointed him.

text.NRSV: David said to Abishai, “Now Sheba son of Bichri will do us more harm than Absalom; take your lord’s servants and pursue him, or he will find fortified cities for himself, and escape from us.”
text.Rotherham: Then said David unto Abishai, Now, shall Sheba son of Bichri, do us more harm than Absolom,—thou, take the servants of thy lord, and pursue him, lest he have got him into fortified cities, and so have escaped our eye.

text.NRSV: Joab’s men went out after him, along with the Cherethites, the Pelethites, and all the warriors; they went out from Jerusalem to pursue Sheba son of Bichri.
text.Rotherham: Then went out after him—the men of Joab, and the Cherethites and the Pelethites, and all the mighty men,—and they went out from Jerusalem, to pursue Sheba son of Bichri.

text.NRSV: When they were at the large stone that is in Gibeon, Amasa came to meet them. Now Joab was wearing a soldier’s garment and over it was a belt with a sword in its sheath fastened at his waist; as he went forward it fell out.
text.Rotherham: When, they, were by the great stone which is in Gibeon, Amasa, had arrived before them. Now, Joab, was girded about with his war-coat as his upper garment, and, over it, a girdle with a sword, fastened upon his loins, in the sheath thereof, and, it, came out and fell.

text.NRSV: Joab said to Amasa, “Is it well with you, my brother?” And Joab took Amasa by the beard with his right hand to kiss him.
text.Rotherham: Then said Joab unto Amasa, Art thou, well, my brother? And Joab took Amasa by the beard with his right hand, to kiss him.

text.NRSV: But Amasa did not notice the sword in Joab’s hand; Joab struck him in the belly so that his entrails poured out on the ground, and he did not strike him again; thus he died. Then Joab and his brother Abishai pursued Sheba son of Bichri.
text.Rotherham: Amasa not heeding the sword that was in the hand of Joab, he smote him therewith in the belly, and shed out his bowels to the ground, and [struck] him not again, and he died. So, Joab and Abishai his brother, pursued Sheba son of Bichri.

text.NRSV: And one of Joab’s men took his stand by Amasa, and said, “Whoever favors Joab, and whoever is for David, let him follow Joab.”
text.Rotherham: Now, a man, stood over him, of the young men of Joab,—and said—Whosoever is well pleased with Joab, and whosoever pertaineth to David, let him follow Joab.

text.NRSV: Amasa lay wallowing in his blood on the highway, and the man saw that all the people were stopping. Since he saw that all who came by him were stopping, he carried Amasa from the highway into a field, and threw a garment over him.
text.Rotherham: But, Amasa, was wallowing in blood, in the midst of the highway,—and, when the man saw that all the people stood still, he moved Amasa, out of the highway into the field, and cast over him a garment, as soon as he saw that every man that came up to him stood still.

text.NRSV: Once he was removed from the highway, all the people went on after Joab to pursue Sheba son of Bichri.
text.Rotherham: When he had removed him out of the highway, every man passed on, after Joab, to pursue Sheba son of Bichri.

text.NRSV: Sheba passed through all the tribes of Israel to Abel of Beth-maacah; and all the Bichrites assembled, and followed him inside.
text.Rotherham: And he passed on throughout all the tribes of Israel, unto Abel and unto Beth-maachah, and all the Berites,—and they were called together, and came in, yea and followed him.

text.NRSV: Joab’s forces came and besieged him in Abel of Beth-maacah; they threw up a siege ramp against the city, and it stood against the rampart. Joab’s forces were battering the wall to bring it down.
text.Rotherham: So they came, and laid siege against him, in Abel, Beth-maachah, and they cast up a mound against the city, so that it stood within a rampart, —and, all the people who were with Joab, were battering the wall to throw it down.

text.NRSV: Then a wise woman called from the city, “Listen! Listen! Tell Joab, ‘Come here, I want to speak to you.’”
text.Rotherham: Then cried a wise woman out of the city,—Hear ye! hear ye! I pray you, say unto Joab, Come near hither, and let me speak unto thee.

text.NRSV: He came near her; and the woman said, “Are you Joab?” He answered, “I am.” Then she said to him, “Listen to the words of your servant.” He answered, “I am listening.”
text.Rotherham: So he came near unto her, and the woman said,—Art thou Joab? And he said,—I am. And she said unto him,—Hear thou the words of thy handmaid. And he said, I do hear.

text.NRSV: Then she said, “They used to say in the old days, ‘Let them inquire at Abel’; and so they would settle a matter.
text.Rotherham: Then spake she, saying,—They, used to speak, in former times, saying, Enquire, in Abel! And, so, they ended it.

text.NRSV: I am one of those who are peaceable and faithful in Israel; you seek to destroy a city that is a mother in Israel; why will you swallow up the heritage of the Lord?”
text.Rotherham: I, am of the peaceable among the faithful in Israel,—thou, art seeking to put to death a city, and a mother in Israel, wherefore wouldst thou swallow up the inheritance of Yahweh?

text.NRSV: Joab answered, “Far be it from me, far be it, that I should swallow up or destroy!
text.Rotherham: Then answered Joab and said,—Far be it! far be it from me! I will neither swallow up nor lay waste.

text.NRSV: That is not the case! But a man of the hill country of Ephraim, called Sheba son of Bichri, has lifted up his hand against King David; give him up alone, and I will withdraw from the city.” The woman said to Joab, “His head shall be thrown over the wall to you.”
text.Rotherham: Not so, is the matter! but, a man of the hill country of Ephraim, Sheba son of Bichri, his name, hath lifted up his hand against the king, even against David, give up him alone, and I will depart from the city. And the woman said unto Joab, Lo! his head, shall be cast unto thee, through the wall.

text.NRSV: Then the woman went to all the people with her wise plan. And they cut off the head of Sheba son of Bichri, and threw it out to Joab. So he blew the trumpet, and they dispersed from the city, and all went to their homes, while Joab returned to Jerusalem to the king.
text.Rotherham: So the woman came unto all the people in her wisdom, and they cut off the head of Sheba son of Bichri, and cast it out unto Joab. And he blew with a horn, and they dispersed themselves from the city, every man to his home; but, Joab, returned to Jerusalem unto the king.

text.NRSV: Joab was over all the army of Israel; Benaiah son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and the Pelethites;
text.Rotherham: And, Joab, was [restored] unto all the army of Israel,—And, Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, was over the Cherethites and over the Pelethites,

text.NRSV: Adoram was in charge of the forced labor; Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the recorder;
text.Rotherham: And, Adoniram, was over the tribute,—And, Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, was the remembrancer;

text.NRSV: Sheva was secretary; Zadok and Abiathar were priests;
text.Rotherham: And, Sheva, was scribe,—And, Zadok and Abiathar, were priests;

text.NRSV: and Ira the Jairite was also David’s priest.
text.Rotherham: Moreover also, Ira the Jairite, was chief ruler unto David.

text.NRSV: Now there was a famine in the days of David for three years, year after year; and David inquired of the Lord. The Lord said, “There is bloodguilt on Saul and on his house, because he put the Gibeonites to death.”
text.Rotherham: And there came to be a famine, in the days of David, for three years, year after year, so then David sought the face of Yahweh,—and Yahweh said—It respecteth Saul and his house, as to bloodshed, in that he put to death the Gibeonites.

text.NRSV: So the king called the Gibeonites and spoke to them. (Now the Gibeonites were not of the people of Israel, but of the remnant of the Amorites; although the people of Israel had sworn to spare them, Saul had tried to wipe them out in his zeal for the people of Israel and Judah.)
text.Rotherham: The king therefore called the Gibeonites, and said unto them (now, the Gibeonites, were, not of the sons of Israel, but of the remnant of the Amorites, with whom, the sons of Israel, had entered into an oath, and Saul had sought to smite them, in his jealousy for the sons of Israel and Judah)—

text.NRSV: David said to the Gibeonites, “What shall I do for you? How shall I make expiation, that you may bless the heritage of the Lord?”
text.Rotherham: wherefore David said unto the Gibeonites—What shall I do for you, and wherewith shall I make propitiation, so that ye may bless the inheritance of Yahweh?

text.NRSV: The Gibeonites said to him, “It is not a matter of silver or gold between us and Saul or his house; neither is it for us to put anyone to death in Israel.” He said, “What do you say that I should do for you?”
text.Rotherham: And the Gibeonites said unto him—It is not a matter with us of silver or gold, with Saul or with his house, neither would we have a man put to death in Israel. And he said,—What do ye say I should do for you?

text.NRSV: They said to the king, “The man who consumed us and planned to destroy us, so that we should have no place in all the territory of Israel—
text.Rotherham: Then said they unto the king, The man who consumed us, and who thought to have destroyed us from taking a place within any of the bounds of Israel,

text.NRSV: let seven of his sons be handed over to us, and we will impale them before the Lord at Gibeon on the mountain of the Lord.” The king said, “I will hand them over.”
text.Rotherham: let there be delivered up to us—seven men of his sons, and we will crucify them unto Yahweh in Gibeah of Saul, the chosen of Yahweh. And the king said, I, will deliver them up.

text.NRSV: But the king spared Mephibosheth, the son of Saul’s son Jonathan, because of the oath of the Lord that was between them, between David and Jonathan son of Saul.
text.Rotherham: But the king had pity upon Mephibosheth, son of Jonathan, Saul’s son,—because of the oath of Yahweh that was between them, between David and Jonathan, Saul’s son.

text.NRSV: The king took the two sons of Rizpah daughter of Aiah, whom she bore to Saul, Armoni and Mephibosheth; and the five sons of Merab daughter of Saul, whom she bore to Adriel son of Barzillai the Meholathite;
text.Rotherham: So the king took the two sons of Rizpah daughter of Aiah, whom she had borne to Saul, even Armoni and Mephibosheth,—and the five sons of Michal daughter of Saul, whom she had borne to Adriel son of Barzillai, the Meholathite;

text.NRSV: he gave them into the hands of the Gibeonites, and they impaled them on the mountain before the Lord. The seven of them perished together. They were put to death in the first days of harvest, at the beginning of barley harvest.
text.Rotherham: and delivered them up into the hand of the Gibeonites, and they crucified them in the mountain, before Yahweh, so they seven fell together,—they being put to death in the first days of harvest, in the beginning of the barley harvest.

text.NRSV: Then Rizpah the daughter of Aiah took sackcloth, and spread it on a rock for herself, from the beginning of harvest until rain fell on them from the heavens; she did not allow the birds of the air to come on the bodies by day, or the wild animals by night.
text.Rotherham: Then Rizpah daughter of Aiah, took sackcloth, and spread it out for herself, on the rock, from the beginning of harvest, until water poured out upon them from the heavens,—and suffered neither the birds of the heavens to rest on them by day, nor the wild beasts of the field, [to devour them] by night.

text.NRSV: When David was told what Rizpah daughter of Aiah, the concubine of Saul, had done,
text.Rotherham: And it was told David,—what Rizpah daughter of Aiah, Saul’s concubine, had done.

text.NRSV: David went and took the bones of Saul and the bones of his son Jonathan from the people of Jabesh-gilead, who had stolen them from the public square of Beth-shan, where the Philistines had hung them up, on the day the Philistines killed Saul on Gilboa.
text.Rotherham: So David went and fetched the bones of Saul, and the bones of Jonathan his son, from the owners of Jabesh-gilead,—who stole them from the broadway of Beth-shan, where the Philistines had hanged them, on the day when the Philistines had smitten Saul in Gilboa;

text.NRSV: He brought up from there the bones of Saul and the bones of his son Jonathan; and they gathered the bones of those who had been impaled.
text.Rotherham: and he brought up from thence the bones of Saul, and the bones of Jonathan his son,—and they gathered together the bones of them who had been crucified;

text.NRSV: They buried the bones of Saul and of his son Jonathan in the land of Benjamin in Zela, in the tomb of his father Kish; they did all that the king commanded. After that, God heeded supplications for the land.
text.Rotherham: so they buried the bones of Saul and Jonathan his son in the land of Benjamin, in Zelah, in the grave of Kish his father, thus did they all that the king commanded,—and God suffered himself to be entreated for the land, after this.

text.NRSV: The Philistines went to war again with Israel, and David went down together with his servants. They fought against the Philistines, and David grew weary.
text.Rotherham: And the Philistines had yet again a war with Israel,—so David went down, and his servants with him, and fought the Philistines, and David became faint.

text.NRSV: Ishbi-benob, one of the descendants of the giants, whose spear weighed three hundred shekels of bronze, and who was fitted out with new weapons, thought to kill David.
text.Rotherham: So, Ishbi-benob, who was of the descendants of the giant, the weight of whose spear-head, was three hundred shekels of bronze, he also being newly armed, thought to smite David;

text.NRSV: But Abishai son of Zeruiah came to his aid, and attacked the Philistine and killed him. Then David’s men swore to him, “You shall not go out with us to battle any longer, so that you do not quench the lamp of Israel.”
text.Rotherham: but Abishai son of Zeruiah, came to his help, and smote the Philistine, and slew him. Then, sware the men of David unto him, saying—Thou must not go forth any more with us, to battle, that thou quench not the lamp of Israel.

text.NRSV: After this a battle took place with the Philistines, at Gob; then Sibbecai the Hushathite killed Saph, who was one of the descendants of the giants.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, after this, that there was yet again a battle in Gob, with the Philistines,—then, Sibbekai the Hushathite smote Saph, who was of the descendants of the giant.

text.NRSV: Then there was another battle with the Philistines at Gob; and Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim, the Bethlehemite, killed Goliath the Gittite, the shaft of whose spear was like a weaver’s beam.
text.Rotherham: And there was yet again a battle in Gob with the Philistines,—when Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim of Bethlehem, smote Goliath the Gittite, the shaft of whose spear was like a weaver’s beam.

text.NRSV: There was again war at Gath, where there was a man of great size, who had six fingers on each hand, and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in number; he too was descended from the giants.
text.Rotherham: And there was yet again a battle in Gath; when there was a man of stature, with six fingers on each hand, and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in number; he also, having been born to the giant;

text.NRSV: When he taunted Israel, Jonathan son of David’s brother Shimei killed him.
text.Rotherham: but, when he reproached Israel, Jonathan son of Shimeah David’s brother, smote, him.

text.NRSV: These four were descended from the giants in Gath; they fell by the hands of David and his servants.
text.Rotherham: These four, had been born to the giant in Gath,—but they fell by the hand of David, and by the hand of his servants.

text.NRSV: David spoke to the Lord the words of this song on the day when the Lord delivered him from the hand of all his enemies, and from the hand of Saul.
text.Rotherham: And David spake unto Yahweh, the words of this song, in the day when Yahweh had rescued him, out of the hand of all his enemies, and out of the hand of Saul;

text.NRSV: He said: The Lord is my rock, my fortress, and my deliverer,
text.Rotherham: and he said,—Yahweh, was my mountain crag and my stronghold, and my deliverer—mine;

text.NRSV: my God, my rock in whom I take refuge, my shield and the horn of my salvation, my stronghold and my refuge, my savior; you save me from violence.
text.Rotherham: My God, was my rock, I sought refuge in him,—My shield, and my horn of salvation, my high tower, and my refuge, My Saviour! from violence, thou didst save me.

text.NRSV: I call upon the Lord, who is worthy to be praised, and I am saved from my enemies.
text.Rotherham: As one worthy to be praised, called I on Yahweh,—And, from my foes, was I saved.

text.NRSV: For the waves of death encompassed me, the torrents of perdition assailed me;
text.Rotherham: When the breakers of death had encompassed me,—the torrents of perdition, made me afraid,—

text.NRSV: the cords of Sheol entangled me, the snares of death confronted me.
text.Rotherham: the meshes of hades, had surrounded me,—the snares of death had confronted me,

text.NRSV: In my distress I called upon the Lord; to my God I called. From his temple he heard my voice, and my cry came to his ears.
text.Rotherham: In my distress, called I on Yahweh, Yea, unto my God, did I call,—and he hearkened, out of his temple, unto my voice, and, my cry for help, was in his ears!

text.NRSV: Then the earth reeled and rocked; the foundations of the heavens trembled and quaked, because he was angry.
text.Rotherham: Then did the earth shake and quake, the foundations of the heavens, were deeply moved,—yea they did shake, because he was angry,

text.NRSV: Smoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth; glowing coals flamed forth from him.
text.Rotherham: There went up a smoke in his nostrils, and, a fire out of his mouth, devoured,—live coals, were kindled from it:

text.NRSV: He bowed the heavens, and came down; thick darkness was under his feet.
text.Rotherham: Then he stretched out the heavens, and came down,—and, thick gloom, was under his feet;

text.NRSV: He rode on a cherub, and flew; he was seen upon the wings of the wind.
text.Rotherham: Then he rode on a cherub, and flew,—and was seen on the wings of the wind;

text.NRSV: He made darkness around him a canopy, thick clouds, a gathering of water.
text.Rotherham: And made of the darkness around him, pavilions,—gathering of waters, clouds of vapours.

text.NRSV: Out of the brightness before him coals of fire flamed forth.
text.Rotherham: Out of the brightness before him, were kindled live coals of fire;

text.NRSV: The Lord thundered from heaven; the Most High uttered his voice.
text.Rotherham: Thunder from the heavens, did Yahweh give forth,—yea, the Highest, uttered his voice;

text.NRSV: He sent out arrows, and scattered them—lightning, and routed them.
text.Rotherham: And he sent forth arrows, and scattered them,—lightning, and confused them;

text.NRSV: Then the channels of the sea were seen, the foundations of the world were laid bare at the rebuke of the Lord, at the blast of the breath of his nostrils.
text.Rotherham: Then appeared the channels of the sea, were uncovered the foundations of the world,—at the rebuke of Yahweh, the blast of the breath of his nostrils;

text.NRSV: He reached from on high, he took me, he drew me out of mighty waters.
text.Rotherham: He sent from on high, he took me,—he drew me out of many waters;

text.NRSV: He delivered me from my strong enemy, from those who hated me; for they were too mighty for me.
text.Rotherham: He rescued me from my foe, in his might,—from them who hated me, because they were too strong for me:

text.NRSV: They came upon me in the day of my calamity, but the Lord was my stay.
text.Rotherham: They confronted me, in the day of my necessity,—then became Yahweh my stay:

text.NRSV: He brought me out into a broad place; he delivered me, because he delighted in me.
text.Rotherham: And brought out, into a large place, even me,—he delivered me, because he delighted in me:

text.NRSV: The Lord rewarded me according to my righteousness; according to the cleanness of my hands he recompensed me.
text.Rotherham: Yahweh rewarded me, according to my righteousness—according to the cleanness of my hands, he repaid me:

text.NRSV: For I have kept the ways of the Lord, and have not wickedly departed from my God.
text.Rotherham: For I had kept the ways of Yahweh,—and not broken away from my God;

text.NRSV: For all his ordinances were before me, and from his statutes I did not turn aside.
text.Rotherham: For, all his regulations, were before me,—and, as for his statutes, I turned not from them.

text.NRSV: I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from guilt.
text.Rotherham: So became I blameless towards him,—and kept myself from mine iniquity:

text.NRSV: Therefore the Lord has recompensed me according to my righteousness, according to my cleanness in his sight.
text.Rotherham: Yahweh therefore repaid me, according to my righteousness,—according to my pureness before his eyes.

text.NRSV: With the loyal you show yourself loyal; with the blameless you show yourself blameless;
text.Rotherham: With the loving, thou didst show thyself loving,—with the blameless hero, thou didst show thyself blameless;

text.NRSV: with the pure you show yourself pure, and with the crooked you show yourself perverse.
text.Rotherham: With the pure, thou didst show thyself pure,—but, with the perverse, thou didst shew thyself ready to contend:

text.NRSV: You deliver a humble people, but your eyes are upon the haughty to bring them down.
text.Rotherham: And, a patient people, thou didst save,—but, thine eyes, were on the lofty—thou layedst them low;

text.NRSV: Indeed, you are my lamp, O Lord, the Lord lightens my darkness.
text.Rotherham: For, thou, wast my lamp, O Yahweh,—and, Yahweh, enlightened my darkness;

text.NRSV: By you I can crush a troop, and by my God I can leap over a wall.
text.Rotherham: For, by thee, I ran through a troop,—by my God, I leapt over a wall.

text.NRSV: This God—his way is perfect; the promise of the Lord proves true; he is a shield for all who take refuge in him.
text.Rotherham: As for God, blameless is his way,—the speech of Yahweh, hath been proved, a shield, he is to all who seek refuge in him.

text.NRSV: For who is God, but the Lord? And who is a rock, except our God?
text.Rotherham: For who is a GOD, save Yahweh? and who a Rock, save our God?

text.NRSV: The God who has girded me with strength has opened wide my path.
text.Rotherham: GOD, is my fortress of strength,—and shewed to the blameless his way;

text.NRSV: He made my feet like the feet of deer, and set me secure on the heights.
text.Rotherham: Planting my feet like the hinds’,—yea, on my high places, he caused me to stand;

text.NRSV: He trains my hands for war, so that my arms can bend a bow of bronze.
text.Rotherham: Teaching my hands, to war,—so that a bow of bronze was bent by mine arms;

text.NRSV: You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your help has made me great.
text.Rotherham: Thus didst thou grant me, as a shield, thy salvation,—and, thy condescension, made me great.

text.NRSV: You enlarged my steps under me, and my feet did not slip.
text.Rotherham: Thou didst widen my stepping-places, under me,—so that mine ankles faltered not:

text.NRSV: I pursued my enemies and destroyed them, and did not turn back until they were consumed.
text.Rotherham: I pursued my foes, and destroyed them,—and returned not, till they were consumed;

text.NRSV: I consumed them; I struck them down, so that they did not rise; they fell under my feet.
text.Rotherham: So I consumed them, and crushed them, and they rose not again,—thus fell they under my feet:

text.NRSV: For you girded me with strength for the battle; you made my assailants sink under me.
text.Rotherham: Thus didst thou gird me with strength, for the battle,—thou subduedst mine assailants under me:

text.NRSV: You made my enemies turn their backs to me, those who hated me, and I destroyed them.
text.Rotherham: And, as for my foes, thou didst give me their neck,—yea, them who hated me, that I might destroy them:

text.NRSV: They looked, but there was no one to save them; they cried to the Lord, but he did not answer them.
text.Rotherham: They cried out, but there was none to save,—unto Yahweh, but he answered them not.

text.NRSV: I beat them fine like the dust of the earth, I crushed them and stamped them down like the mire of the streets.
text.Rotherham: Then did I beat them in pieces, like the dust of the earth,—like the clay of the lanes, did I crush them, stamp them down.

text.NRSV: You delivered me from strife with the peoples; you kept me as the head of the nations; people whom I had not known served me.
text.Rotherham: Thus didst thou rescue me from the contentions of my people, didst keep me to be the head of nations:—a people whom I had not known, served me;

text.NRSV: Foreigners came cringing to me; as soon as they heard of me, they obeyed me.
text.Rotherham: The sons of the foreigner, came cringing unto me,—at the hearing of the ear, they submitted to me,—

text.NRSV: Foreigners lost heart, and came trembling out of their strongholds.
text.Rotherham: The sons of the foreigner, were disheartened, and came quaking, out of their fortresses.

text.NRSV: The Lord lives! Blessed be my rock, and exalted be my God, the rock of my salvation,
text.Rotherham: Yahweh liveth, and blessed be my rock,—and exalted be the God (of the rock) of my salvation:—

text.NRSV: the God who gave me vengeance and brought down peoples under me,
text.Rotherham: The GOD who hath avenged me, and brought down peoples under me;

text.NRSV: who brought me out from my enemies; you exalted me above my adversaries, you delivered me from the violent.
text.Rotherham: And brought me forth from among my foes,—yea, from mine assailants, hast thou set me on high, from the man of violence, hast thou delivered me.

text.NRSV: For this I will extol you, O Lord, among the nations, and sing praises to your name.
text.Rotherham: For this cause, will I praise thee, O Yahweh, among the nations,—and, to thy name, will I touch the strings:—

text.NRSV: Great triumphs he gives to his king, and shows steadfast love to his anointed, to David and his descendants forever.
text.Rotherham: Who hath made great the victories of his King,—and shown lovingkindness to his Anointed One, to David and to his Seed, unto times age-abiding.

text.NRSV: Now these are the last words of David: The oracle of David, son of Jesse, the oracle of the man whom God exalted, the anointed of the God of Jacob, the favorite of the Strong One of Israel:
text.Rotherham: Now, these, are the last words of David,—The oracle of David, son of Jesse, Yea the oracle of The man raised up on high, The Anointed of the God of Jacob, the Delight of the Songs of Israel:

text.NRSV: The spirit of the Lord speaks through me, his word is upon my tongue.
text.Rotherham: The Spirit of Yahweh, spake in me,—And, his word, was on my tongue;

text.NRSV: The God of Israel has spoken, the Rock of Israel has said to me: One who rules over people justly, ruling in the fear of God,
text.Rotherham: Said the God of Israel, Unto me, spake the Rock of Israel:—One Ruling over Men, A Righteous One, ruling in the reverence of God,

text.NRSV: is like the light of morning, like the sun rising on a cloudless morning, gleaming from the rain on the grassy land.
text.Rotherham: Is even as the light of the morning when ariseth the sun,—A morning, without clouds, [As] from brightness, [and] from rain, the fresh shoots out of the earth.

text.NRSV: Is not my house like this with God? For he has made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things and secure. Will he not cause to prosper all my help and my desire?
text.Rotherham: When, not so, was my house with GOD, Then, a covenant age-abiding, he appointed me, Ordered in all things and guarded, Now that it is all my salvation and all my desire, Will he not make it shoot forth?

text.NRSV: But the godless are all like thorns that are thrown away; for they cannot be picked up with the hand;
text.Rotherham: But, as for the abandoned, like thorns to be tossed away are they all,—For, not with the hand, can they be taken;

text.NRSV: to touch them one uses an iron bar or the shaft of a spear. And they are entirely consumed in fire on the spot.
text.Rotherham: But, the man that would touch them, Must fence himself with iron, and the shaft of a spear,—Then, with fire, shall they be, consumed, on the spot!

text.NRSV: These are the names of the warriors whom David had: Josheb-basshebeth a Tahchemonite; he was chief of the Three; he wielded his spear against eight hundred whom he killed at one time.
text.Rotherham: These, are the names of the mighty men, who belonged to David,—The president a Tachmonite head of the charioteers, the same, was Adino the Eznite, for eight hundred, slain at one time.

text.NRSV: Next to him among the three warriors was Eleazar son of Dodo son of Ahohi. He was with David when they defied the Philistines who were gathered there for battle. The Israelites withdrew,
text.Rotherham: And, after him, Eleazar son of Dodo, son of Ahohi,—in the hero-class of mighty men, with David, when they reproached the Philistines, they were gathered together there to battle, but the men of Israel had gone up;

text.NRSV: but he stood his ground. He struck down the Philistines until his arm grew weary, though his hand clung to the sword. The Lord brought about a great victory that day. Then the people came back to him—but only to strip the dead.
text.Rotherham: he, however, arose and smote among the Philistines until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto his sword, so Yahweh wrought a great victory on that day,—the people, coming back after him, only to strip the slain.

text.NRSV: Next to him was Shammah son of Agee, the Hararite. The Philistines gathered together at Lehi, where there was a plot of ground full of lentils; and the army fled from the Philistines.
text.Rotherham: And, after him, Shammah son of Agee, the Hararite,—and, when the Philistines were gathered together into a troop, there being at hand an allotment of field-land full of lentils, but, the people, having fled from the face of the Philistines,

text.NRSV: But he took his stand in the middle of the plot, defended it, and killed the Philistines; and the Lord brought about a great victory.
text.Rotherham: then took he his stand in the midst of the allotment, and defended it, and smote the Philistines,—and Yahweh wrought a great victory.

text.NRSV: Towards the beginning of harvest three of the thirty chiefs went down to join David at the cave of Adullam, while a band of Philistines was encamped in the valley of Rephaim.
text.Rotherham: And three of the thirty chiefs descended, and came in, towards harvest, unto David, unto the cave of Adullam,—although, a troop of Philistines, were encamped in the vale of Rephaim;

text.NRSV: David was then in the stronghold; and the garrison of the Philistines was then at Bethlehem.
text.Rotherham: and, David, then was in a stronghold,—and, a garrison of Philistines, was then in Bethlehem.

text.NRSV: David said longingly, “O that someone would give me water to drink from the well that is by the gate of Bethlehem!”
text.Rotherham: And David longed, and said,—Who will give me to drink water out of the well of Bethlehem, that is within the gate?

text.NRSV: Then the three warriors broke through the camp of the Philistines, drew water from the well that is by the gate of Bethlehem, and brought it to David. But he would not drink of it; he poured it out to the Lord,
text.Rotherham: And the three mighty men brake through the camp of Philistines, and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem, that was within the gate, and bare it, and brought it in unto David,—yet would he not drink, but poured it out unto Yahweh,

text.NRSV: for he said, “The Lord forbid that I should do this. Can I drink the blood of the men who went at the risk of their lives?” Therefore he would not drink it. The three warriors did these things.
text.Rotherham: and said—Be it far from me, O Yahweh, that I should do this! is it not the blood of the men who went with their lives [in their hands] ? So he would not drink it. These things, did the three mighty men.

text.NRSV: Now Abishai son of Zeruiah, the brother of Joab, was chief of the Thirty. With his spear he fought against three hundred men and killed them, and won a name beside the Three.
text.Rotherham: And. Abishai brother of Joab son of Zeruiah, he, was chief of three, in that he brandished his spear against three hundred whom he slew; and, he, had a name among three.

text.NRSV: He was the most renowned of the Thirty, and became their commander; but he did not attain to the Three.
text.Rotherham: Was he not most honourable, of the three, and so became their captain? Nevertheless, unto the three, he attained not.

text.NRSV: Benaiah son of Jehoiada was a valiant warrior from Kabzeel, a doer of great deeds; he struck down two sons of Ariel of Moab. He also went down and killed a lion in a pit on a day when snow had fallen.
text.Rotherham: Benaiah also, son of Jehoiadah, son of an active man, hero of many a deed, a man of Kabzeel, he, smote the two sons of Ariel of Moab, he, also went down and smote a lion in the midst of a pit, on a day of snow;

text.NRSV: And he killed an Egyptian, a handsome man. The Egyptian had a spear in his hand; but Benaiah went against him with a staff, snatched the spear out of the Egyptian’s hand, and killed him with his own spear.
text.Rotherham: he, also smote an Egyptian, who was of valiant bearing, and, in the hand of the Egyptian, was a spear, but he went down unto him with a staff,—and wrested the spear out of the hand of the Egyptian, and slew him with his own spear.

text.NRSV: Such were the things Benaiah son of Jehoiada did, and won a name beside the three warriors.
text.Rotherham: These things, did Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and, he, had a name among three mighty men.

text.NRSV: He was renowned among the Thirty, but he did not attain to the Three. And David put him in charge of his bodyguard.
text.Rotherham: He was the most honourable, of thirty, although, unto the three, he attained no,—so David added him to his council.

text.NRSV: Among the Thirty were Asahel brother of Joab; Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem;
text.Rotherham: Asahel, brother of Joab, was among the thirty,—Elhanan, son of Dodo, of Bethlehem;

text.NRSV: Shammah of Harod; Elika of Harod;
text.Rotherham: Shammah, the Harodite; Elika, the Harodite;

text.NRSV: Helez the Paltite; Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa;
text.Rotherham: Helez, the Paltite, Ira, son of Ikkesh, the Tekoite;

text.NRSV: Abiezer of Anathoth; Mebunnai the Hushathite;
text.Rotherham: Abiezer, the Anathothite, Mebunnai, the Hushathite;

text.NRSV: Zalmon the Ahohite; Maharai of Netophah;
text.Rotherham: Zalmon, the Ahohite, Maharai, the Netophathite;

text.NRSV: Heleb son of Baanah of Netophah; Ittai son of Ribai of Gibeah of the Benjaminites;
text.Rotherham: Heleb, son of Baanah, the Netophathite,—Ittai, son of Ribai, of Gibeah, of the sons of Benjamin;

text.NRSV: Benaiah of Pirathon; Hiddai of Gaash;
text.Rotherham: Benaiah, a Pirathonite, Hiddai, of the torrents of Gaash;

text.NRSV: Abi-albon the Arbathite; Azmaveth of Bahurim;
text.Rotherham: Abi-albon, the Arbathite, Azmaveth, the Barhumite;

text.NRSV: Eliahba of Shaalbon; the sons of Jashen: Jonathan,
text.Rotherham: Eliahba, the Shaalbonite, (Of) the sons of Jashen, Jonathan;

text.NRSV: son of Shammah the Hararite; Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite;
text.Rotherham: Shammah, the Hararite, Ahiam, son of Sharar, the Ararite;

text.NRSV: Eliphelet son of Ahasbai of Maacah; Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilonite;
text.Rotherham: Eliphelet, son of Ahasbai, son of the Maacathite. Eliam, son of Ahithophel, the Gilonite.

text.NRSV: Hezro of Carmel; Paarai the Arbite;
text.Rotherham: Hezro, the Carmelite, Paarai, the Arbite;

text.NRSV: Igal son of Nathan of Zobah; Bani the Gadite;
text.Rotherham: Igal, son of Nathan, of Zobah, Bani, the Gadite;

text.NRSV: Zelek the Ammonite; Naharai of Beeroth, the armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah;
text.Rotherham: Zelek, the Ammonite,—Naharai, the Beerothite, armour bearers to Joab son of Zeruiah;

text.NRSV: Ira the Ithrite; Gareb the Ithrite;
text.Rotherham: Ira, the Ithrite, Gareb, the Ithrite;

text.NRSV: Uriah the Hittite—thirty-seven in all.
text.Rotherham: Uriah, the Hittite, In all, thirty and seven.

text.NRSV: Again the anger of the Lord was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, “Go, count the people of Israel and Judah.”
text.Rotherham: And again was the anger of Yahweh kindled against Israel,—so that he suffered David to be moved against them, saying, Go, count Israel and Judah.

text.NRSV: So the king said to Joab and the commanders of the army, who were with him, “Go through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan to Beer-sheba, and take a census of the people, so that I may know how many there are.”
text.Rotherham: The king, therefore, said unto Joab, captain of the force, who was with him—Go to and fro, I pray thee, throughout all the tribes of Israel, from Dan even unto Beer -sheba, and number ye the people,—so shall I know the sum of the people.

text.NRSV: But Joab said to the king, “May the Lord your God increase the number of the people a hundredfold, while the eyes of my lord the king can still see it! But why does my lord the king want to do this?”
text.Rotherham: Then said Joab unto the king—Yea, Yahweh thy God add unto the people, how many soever they be, a hundredfold, and [suffer] the eyes of my lord the king, to see it,—but, my lord the king, wherefore doth he find pleasure in this thing?

text.NRSV: But the king’s word prevailed against Joab and the commanders of the army. So Joab and the commanders of the army went out from the presence of the king to take a census of the people of Israel.
text.Rotherham: Notwithstanding, the word of the king prevailed against Joab, and over the captains of the force,—so Joab went forth, with the captains of the force, before the king, to number the people—Israel.

text.NRSV: They crossed the Jordan, and began from Aroer and from the city that is in the middle of the valley, toward Gad and on to Jazer.
text.Rotherham: And they passed over the Jordan,—and encamped in Aroer, on the right side of the city, that is in the midst of the ravine of Gad, even towards Jazer.

text.NRSV: Then they came to Gilead, and to Kadesh in the land of the Hittites; and they came to Dan, and from Dan they went around to Sidon,
text.Rotherham: Thus came they to Gilead, and unto the land of Tahtim-hodshi,—and came to Dan-jaan, and round about Zidon;

text.NRSV: and came to the fortress of Tyre and to all the cities of the Hivites and Canaanites; and they went out to the Negeb of Judah at Beer-sheba.
text.Rotherham: and entered the fortress of Tyre, and all the cities of the Hivites, and of the Canaanites,—and they went out to the South of Judah, even to Beer-sheba.

text.NRSV: So when they had gone through all the land, they came back to Jerusalem at the end of nine months and twenty days.
text.Rotherham: So, when they had gone to and fro throughout all the land, they came, at the end of nine months and twenty days, unto Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Joab reported to the king the number of those who had been recorded: in Israel eight hundred thousand soldiers able to draw the sword, and those of Judah were five hundred thousand.
text.Rotherham: And Joab delivered up the sum of the number of the people, unto the king,—and there were found to be, in Israel, eight hundred thousand men of valour, drawing the sword,—and, the men of Judah, five hundred thousand men.

text.NRSV: But afterward, David was stricken to the heart because he had numbered the people. David said to the Lord, “I have sinned greatly in what I have done. But now, O Lord, I pray you, take away the guilt of your servant; for I have done very foolishly.”
text.Rotherham: And the heart of David smote him, after he had reckoned up the people,—and David said unto Yahweh—I have sinned greatly, in what I have done, Now, therefore, O Yahweh, take away, I beseech thee, the iniquity of thy servant, for I have done very foolishly.

text.NRSV: When David rose in the morning, the word of the Lord came to the prophet Gad, David’s seer, saying,
text.Rotherham: And, when David arose in the morning, the word of Yahweh, had come unto Gad the prophet, the seer of David, saying:

text.NRSV: “Go and say to David: Thus says the Lord: Three things I offer you; choose one of them for me to do to you.”
text.Rotherham: Go and speak unto David—Thus, saith Yahweh, Three things, do I offer thee,—choose thee one of them, that I may do it unto thee.

text.NRSV: So Gad came to David and told him; he asked him, “Shall three years of famine come to you on your land? Or will you flee three months before your foes while they pursue you? Or shall there be three days’ pestilence in your land? Now consider, and decide what answer I shall return to the one who sent me.”
text.Rotherham: So Gad came in unto David, and told him,—and said to him: Shall there come unto thee seven years of famine in thy land? Or, for three months, wilt thou flee before thine enemies while, they, pursue thee? Or shall there be, for three days, pestilence in thy land? Now, consider and see, what, answer, I shall return to him that sent me.

text.NRSV: Then David said to Gad, “I am in great distress; let us fall into the hand of the Lord, for his mercy is great; but let me not fall into human hands.”
text.Rotherham: And David said unto Gad—I am in a great strait,—let us fall, I pray thee, into the hand of Yahweh, for, manifold, are, his compassions, but, into the hand of man, let me not fall.

text.NRSV: So the Lord sent a pestilence on Israel from that morning until the appointed time; and seventy thousand of the people died, from Dan to Beer-sheba.
text.Rotherham: So Yahweh sent forth a pestilence throughout Israel, from the morning even unto the time appointed,—and there died of the people, from Dan even unto Beer-sheba, seventy thousand men.

text.NRSV: But when the angel stretched out his hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it, the Lord relented concerning the evil, and said to the angel who was bringing destruction among the people, “It is enough; now stay your hand.” The angel of the Lord was then by the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.
text.Rotherham: But, when the messenger stretched out his hand towards Jerusalem, to destroy it, then relented Yahweh as to the evil, and he said to the messenger who was destroying the people—Enough! now, stay thy hand. And, the messenger of Yahweh, was by the threshing-floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

text.NRSV: When David saw the angel who was destroying the people, he said to the Lord, “I alone have sinned, and I alone have done wickedly; but these sheep, what have they done? Let your hand, I pray, be against me and against my father’s house.”
text.Rotherham: Then said David unto Yahweh, when he saw the messenger who was smiting the people, yea he said—Lo! I, have sinned, and, I, have done perversely, but what have, these sheep, done? Let thy hand, I pray thee, be against me, and against the house of my father!

text.NRSV: Gad came to David that day and said to him, “Go up and erect an altar to the Lord on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.”
text.Rotherham: And Gad came unto David, on that day,—and said unto him—Go up, rear thou unto Yahweh an altar, in the threshing-floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

text.NRSV: Following Gad’s instructions, David went up, as the Lord had commanded.
text.Rotherham: So David went up, according to the word of Gad, as Yahweh had commanded.

text.NRSV: When Araunah looked down, he saw the king and his servants coming toward him; and Araunah went out and prostrated himself before the king with his face to the ground.
text.Rotherham: And Araunah looked out, and saw the king, and his servants, passing over unto him—so Araunah went forth, and did homage unto the king, with his face to the ground.

text.NRSV: Araunah said, “Why has my lord the king come to his servant?” David said, “To buy the threshing floor from you, in order to build an altar to the Lord, so that the plague may be averted from the people.”
text.Rotherham: Then said Araunah, Wherefore, hath my lord the king come unto his servant? And David said—To buy of thee the threshing-floor, to build an altar unto Yahweh, that the plague may be stayed from off the people.

text.NRSV: Then Araunah said to David, “Let my lord the king take and offer up what seems good to him; here are the oxen for the burnt offering, and the threshing sledges and the yokes of the oxen for the wood.
text.Rotherham: Then said Araunah unto David, Let my lord the king accept it and cause to ascend what is good in his own eyes,—see! the oxen for the ascending-sacrifice, and the threshing-sledges and ox-yokes for wood.

text.NRSV: All this, O king, Araunah gives to the king.” And Araunah said to the king, “May the Lord your God respond favorably to you.”
text.Rotherham: The whole, did Araunah give, as a king to a king. And Araunah said unto the king, Yahweh thy God, accept thee!

text.NRSV: But the king said to Araunah, “No, but I will buy them from you for a price; I will not offer burnt offerings to the Lord my God that cost me nothing.” So David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
text.Rotherham: But the king said unto Araunah—Nay! but I will, buy, it of thee, for a price, and will not cause to ascend unto Yahweh my God, offerings that have cost me nothing. So David bought the threshing-floor, and the oxen, for fifty shekels of silver;

text.NRSV: David built there an altar to the Lord, and offered burnt offerings and offerings of well-being. So the Lord answered his supplication for the land, and the plague was averted from Israel.
text.Rotherham: and David built there an altar unto Yahweh, and caused to go up ascending-sacrifices and peace-offerings,—then was Yahweh entreated for the land, and the plague was stayed from Israel.
